Definition: A programming paradigm is a style or way of programming that provides a distinct approach to solving problems.
Content: Programming paradigms describe fundamental approaches to designing and structuring code. Common paradigms include imperative (step-by-step commands), functional (computation as evaluation of functions), and object-oriented (modeling real-world entities as objects).
Learning-Goal: Students will be able to identify and describe different programming paradigms.
Common-Misconceptions: One paradigm is always better than all others
All modern languages are purely object-oriented
Question-Prompts: Name three programming paradigms and briefly describe each.
Which paradigm focuses on functions as first-class citizens?
References: Concepts of Programming Languages by Robert Sebesta

Definition: OOP is a programming paradigm where solutions are modeled as interacting objects with state and behavior.
Content: In OOP, you design software by modeling real-world concepts as objects that contain data (state) and methods (behavior). Objects communicate via message passing. OOP emphasizes encapsulation, abstraction, inheritance, and polymorphism.
Learning-Goal: Students will be able to explain the core idea of OOP and identify its key principles.
Common-Misconceptions: OOP is just about using classes
All problems are best solved with OOP
Question-Prompts: What are the four pillars of OOP?
How does OOP differ from imperative programming?
References: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software by Gamma et al.

Definition: OOD is the process of planning and structuring software using object-oriented principles before coding.
Content: OOD focuses on analyzing requirements and modeling systems using classes, objects, and their relationships. It emphasizes reusability, modularity, and maintainability. Tools like UML diagrams are often used in OOD.
Learning-Goal: Students will be able to create basic object-oriented designs from problem descriptions.
Common-Misconceptions: OOD is the same as OOP
OOD requires UML diagrams
OOD means just drawing classes
Question-Prompts: What is the difference between OOD and OOP?
Why is OOD important before writing code?
References: Object-Oriented Analysis and Design with Applications by Grady Booch

Definition: A class is a blueprint for creating objects, defining their state (fields/properties) and behavior (methods).
Content: Classes encapsulate data and functions into a single unit. An object is an instance of a class. Classes enable abstraction, encapsulation, and code reuse. In many languages, they support inheritance and polymorphism.
Examples: Java Class with fields and methods
Learning-Goal: Students will be able to define a class and create objects from it.
Common-Misconceptions: A class is the same as an object
You can use methods without creating an object (unless static)
Question-Prompts: What is the relationship between a class and an object?
Give an example of a simple class in your preferred language.

Definition: An object is an instance of a class, representing a specific entity with its own state and behavior.
Content: Objects are created from classes and contain actual values for fields and methods that can act on those values. Objects interact by sending messages (method calls).
Examples: A Car object created from a Car class
A User object in a web application
Learning-Goal: Students will be able to instantiate objects from classes and explain the role of an object in OOP.
Common-Misconceptions: Objects and classes are the same thing
All objects must directly model real-world entities
Question-Prompts: What is the difference between a class and an object?
Give an example of an object and its state/behavior.
References: Fundamentals of OOP in C#, Java, Python tutorials
